---
title: "Find the Bottleneck, Build What Matters"
status: mature
categories: [Mental Model]
---

*A practical guide to using structured problem-solving — inspired by Elon Musk's "Algorithm" — to decide what agentic workflows your team should actually build.*

---

**Transparency note:** This post was generated by Claude (Anthropic) and reviewed by a human editor for accuracy, tone, and relevance. All factual claims about the Algorithm's origin have been verified against primary and secondary sources, which are cited inline. Where interpretation or adaptation extends beyond the original framework, we've noted it.

---

## The Most Expensive Mistake in Automation

Here's a pattern that plays out in every organization building agentic workflows: someone identifies a repetitive process, builds a clever automation around it, ships it — and then realizes the process shouldn't have existed in the first place.

It sounds obvious in retrospect. But it happens constantly, because the instinct to build is strong, and the discipline to question first is rare.

This post is about a framework that guards against that mistake. It's a structured, five-step method for finding the one constraint that matters most in any system — and then deciding whether to question it, delete it, simplify it, speed it up, or automate it. In that order. Always in that order.

The framework didn't originate in software or analytics. It came from a factory floor during one of the most painful scaling episodes in modern manufacturing history.

## Where the Algorithm Came From

In 2017 and 2018, Tesla was trying to ramp production of the Model 3 from a few hundred cars per week to 5,000. It was not going well. Elon Musk described the period as "production hell" — he was sleeping on a couch at the Fremont factory, wearing the same clothes for days, trying to diagnose why the line kept stalling.

The root cause, as Musk later admitted, was that he had tried to automate everything from the start. Tesla had pursued what he internally called the "alien dreadnought" — a fully robotic assembly line — and it backfired. Robots couldn't handle the variability of the real world. The line was slower with automation than it would have been with humans.

Out of that experience, Musk distilled what he simply calls "the Algorithm" — five steps, applied in strict sequence, that he began repeating in every production meeting. He first shared the framework publicly during a Starbase tour with Tim Dodd (the Everyday Astronaut) in July 2021, and Walter Isaacson later documented it in detail in his 2023 biography.

> **Fact check:** The Everyday Astronaut interview was filmed on July 30, 2021 at SpaceX's Starbase facility in Boca Chica, Texas, and published as a three-part YouTube series starting August 3, 2021. The Algorithm section begins around the 13-minute mark. Isaacson's biography, *Elon Musk*, was published September 12, 2023 by Simon & Schuster and contains the most detailed written account of the Algorithm and its corollaries. The Model 3 "production hell" period is well-documented across Bloomberg, TechCrunch, and Tesla's own quarterly reports from 2017–2018.

There's a story from that period that captures why the step order matters. During Model 3 production, Musk noticed the battery pack contained a fiberglass mat. The team had spent significant time and money automating the process of installing it. When Musk asked what the mat was for, he was told it was for noise reduction. When he went to the noise reduction engineers, they said it was no longer needed — they'd found another solution — but had forgotten to tell the battery team.

The team had optimized, accelerated, and automated the installation of a part that shouldn't have existed. That's the mistake the Algorithm is designed to prevent.

> **Fact check:** The battery mat story is documented in multiple sources, including Ray Hightower's detailed summary of Isaacson's biography and the ModelThinkers analysis of Musk's five-step process. Hightower's account specifies that Musk observed the extra plastic piece while working the assembly line himself, traced it to noise reduction engineers, and discovered the requirement was outdated.

## Why This Matters for Your Team

You probably aren't building rockets or cars. But the underlying pattern is identical in any knowledge work operation — and especially in teams building agentic workflows.

Consider how most teams approach automation today: someone identifies a task that feels repetitive, builds an agent or workflow to handle it, and declares victory. The problem is that nobody asked whether the task should exist in the first place, whether two upstream steps could be collapsed, or whether the entire process was a vestige of a context that no longer applies.

The Algorithm provides a structured sequence for asking those questions *before* you build. It's not complicated. But the discipline of following the steps in order — and resisting the pull to jump straight to automation — is where most of the value lives.

## The Five Steps

The steps must be followed in sequence. Each one earns the right to the next. The most common failure mode is jumping to Step 5 before doing Steps 1 and 2.

### Step 1: Question Every Requirement

Every requirement should trace back to a real person with a real reason — not "the legal team said so" or "we've always done it this way."

As Musk puts it in the Isaacson biography: "Each should come with the name of the person who made it. You should never accept that a requirement came from a department." The reason is practical: you can't have a conversation with a department. You can have a conversation with a person, and that person can tell you whether the requirement still makes sense.

Requirements from experienced, respected people are the hardest to question — and often the most important to revisit. Nobody pushes back on the senior engineer or the VP who established a process three years ago. But contexts change, and requirements that were smart in one context can become drag in another.

**Questions to ask your team:**

- Who specifically asked for this step? What's their name?
- What happens if we just stop doing this?
- Would someone joining the team today, with fresh eyes, include this?
- Is this requirement left over from a context that no longer applies?
- Are we doing this because it's necessary, or because it's comfortable?

**The trap to watch for:** Skipping this step entirely and jumping straight to "How do I automate this?" This is the most common and most expensive mistake.

### Step 2: Delete Before You Build

After questioning, the next step is to identify what you can cut entirely. Not optimize, not simplify — delete. Remove whole steps, whole reports, whole approval layers.

Musk's guidance here is deliberately aggressive: "You may have to add them back later. In fact, if you do not end up adding back at least 10% of them, then you didn't delete enough."

> **Fact check:** The "add back at least 10%" guideline is directly quoted from Isaacson's biography and is consistent across the Everyday Astronaut interview and multiple secondary sources. Musk frames this as a calibration mechanism — if you never add anything back, you're being too conservative with deletion.

A useful litmus test: when was the last time your team added back something it previously removed? If the answer is "never," you probably haven't been bold enough.

The key insight is reversibility. Most process changes are two-way doors — if you delete a step and it turns out you need it, you can add it back. The bar for removing reversible things should be very low.

**Questions to ask your team:**

- Which steps can we simply stop doing?
- What's the minimum viable version of this process?
- What are we doing "just in case" that's never actually been needed?
- If we deleted this and it was a mistake, how hard would it be to add back?

**The trap to watch for:** Tweaking a step that shouldn't exist. Polishing something that should be removed.

### Step 3: Simplify and Optimize

Only after you've questioned and deleted do you earn the right to optimize. This is the step most people want to start with — and that's exactly why it's Step 3.

As Musk describes the error: "It's possibly the most common error of a smart engineer to optimize a thing that should not exist. Everyone's been trained in high school and college that you answer the question. You can't tell the professor your question is dumb. So everyone has this mental straightjacket on and they'll work on optimizing the thing that should simply not exist."

> **Fact check:** This quote is from the Everyday Astronaut interview (Summer 2021) and is also cited in the Startup Archive transcript. The "mental straightjacket" phrasing is Musk's.

Now take what survived Steps 1 and 2 and make it as clean as possible. Merge steps, reduce complexity, lower the skill bar so that anyone on the team can run it without specialized training.

**Questions to ask your team:**

- Can any remaining steps be combined?
- Is there a simpler tool or method that gets 90% of the result?
- Where is precision higher than the decision actually requires?
- Could a new team member run this on day one without training?

**The trap to watch for:** Over-engineering a solution with bells and whistles nobody asked for.

### Step 4: Accelerate Cycle Time

With a simplified system, you can now focus on speed — but speed means removing wait time, not pressuring people to work faster.

Most processes are 80% or more waiting and 20% actual work. The biggest gains come from compressing feedback loops, eliminating queues (approval queues, review backlogs, meeting cadences), and reducing batch sizes.

Musk's caution here is memorable: "If you're digging your grave, don't dig it faster." Only accelerate after you've confirmed you're moving in the right direction.

> **Fact check:** The "digging your grave" quote appears in the Everyday Astronaut interview and is cited across multiple secondary sources including Super Engineer and ModelThinkers.

A useful thought experiment: "If this had to ship by Friday instead of next month, what would we cut?" The things you'd cut are probably things you should cut anyway.

**Questions to ask your team:**

- How long does one full cycle take? What's the wait-to-work ratio?
- Where are the hidden queues?
- Can we run smaller batches more frequently?
- What breaks first if we cut the timeline in half? (That thing is the real constraint.)

**The trap to watch for:** Pressuring people to work faster instead of removing the structural delays that slow them down.

### Step 5: Automate — Only Now

Now — and only now — you've earned the right to automate. You've removed the unnecessary, simplified the essential, and compressed the timeline. Automating at this point amplifies a clean system instead of scaling a messy one.

As Musk reflects in the Isaacson biography: "The big mistake in Nevada and at Fremont was that I began by trying to automate every step. We should have waited until all the requirements had been questioned, parts and processes deleted, and the bugs were shaken out."

> **Fact check:** This quote is from Isaacson's biography and describes the Tesla Gigafactory in Nevada and the Fremont factory. Musk has consistently cited premature automation as his single biggest manufacturing mistake.

A good rule for knowledge work: automate routers, keep decision-makers. If someone's job in a process is passing things from A to B based on simple rules, that's a workflow — not a judgment call — and it's a strong candidate for an agentic automation. If the step requires genuine judgment, context, or relationship management, keep a human in the loop.

**Questions to ask your team:**

- What's the most repetitive manual task that survived Steps 1–4?
- Is there a person acting as a router — not a decision-maker — who could be replaced?
- What would break if this ran unsupervised?
- Can you automate the monitoring even if you can't automate the task itself?

**The trap to watch for:** Automating a broken process. You'll just produce broken outputs faster — and with more confidence.

## How to Run This With Your Team

The Algorithm is most useful as a structured diagnostic session. Here's how to run one in three phases.

### Phase 1: Map the System

Before diagnosing anything, map the topology. Every system is a chain — a funnel, a pipeline, a workflow, a sequence of decisions.

Define the goal (a single metric), identify each sequential step from input to outcome, and estimate throughput or cycle time at each stage. If you lack data, that absence is itself a finding — you can't improve what you can't measure.

The stage with the worst relative throughput, longest cycle time, or biggest drop-off is the candidate bottleneck.

### Phase 2: Run the Algorithm on the Bottleneck

Apply all five steps — in order — to the identified bottleneck stage. Not to the whole system. Not to the stage that's most fun to optimize. To the bottleneck.

This is the core insight from the Theory of Constraints: improving a non-bottleneck doesn't improve the system. It just creates the illusion of progress.

### Phase 3: Synthesize and Act

Produce a clear diagnosis: the bottleneck, the root cause (traced to a specific requirement or structural issue), the single highest-leverage next move, and where the bottleneck will likely shift once the current constraint is resolved.

The output should be a concrete action to take this week — not a roadmap for next quarter.

## Applying This Across Domains

The framework translates to any system with sequential stages. Here's how the chain, common bottleneck, and opening question map to several common domains:

**Analytics / BI:** The chain runs from Collect through Transform, Analyze, Present, Decide, to Act. The most common bottleneck is the Decide-to-Act gap — analysis is done, but nobody acts on it. The opening question: "Why do we produce this report?"

**Experimentation:** Ideate through Design, Build, Run, Analyze, Decide, to Ship. The bottleneck is often at Design/Build or Decide/Ship handoffs. The opening question: "Why does this need 95% confidence?"

**Team Operations:** Request through Prioritize, Assign, Execute, Review, to Ship. The bottleneck is almost always the Review/Approval stage. The opening question: "Who required this approval layer?"

**Product / Growth:** Traffic through Land, Consider, Buy, Activate, to Retain. The bottleneck is the worst conversion drop-off. The opening question: "Why do we ask for X on this page?"

## Operating Principles

Six principles that make this framework work in practice:

**Be curious, not critical.** When you question a requirement, you're not questioning the person who made it. You're honoring everyone's time by making sure every step earns its place. "Help me understand why we do it this way" goes further than "Why are we doing this?"

**One bottleneck at a time.** It's tempting to fix everything at once. Resist that urge. Improving a non-bottleneck doesn't improve the system. Find the one constraint. Fix it. Then find the next one.

**Let the numbers lead.** Use data when you have it. When you don't, that absence is itself a finding. Sometimes the most valuable first step is establishing a baseline.

**Enforce the order.** If someone wants to jump to automation, gently bring them back: "Before we automate this, let's first ask — should this step exist at all?" The sequence isn't bureaucracy. It prevents you from scaling problems.

**Favor two-way doors.** Most decisions are reversible. If you can delete a step and add it back later without major cost, just delete it and see what happens. Save your careful analysis for one-way doors.

**Name names, kindly.** Tracing a requirement to "the team" or "leadership" makes it unkillable. Tracing it to a specific person makes it a conversation you can actually have — about whether this still makes sense.

## What This Doesn't Cover

The Algorithm is a diagnostic and prioritization framework. It tells you *where* to focus and *what order* to work in. It doesn't replace domain expertise in the actual building. Once you've identified the right thing to automate, you still need good engineering, good testing, and good judgment about where to keep humans in the loop.

It's also worth noting that the framework emerged from manufacturing — a domain where the systems are physical, the feedback loops are visible, and "delete a part" is a literal, tangible action. In knowledge work and analytics, the "parts" are often invisible: a column in a report nobody reads, a stakeholder review that's become ceremonial, a data transformation that made sense two migrations ago. The translation requires honesty about which parts of your process are load-bearing and which are vestigial.

## The Bottom Line

The goal isn't to automate everything. It's to make sure everything that exists deserves to.

Before you build your next agentic workflow, run the Algorithm. Question the requirements. Delete what you can. Simplify what survives. Speed up the cycle. And only then — automate.

You might find that the most valuable thing you build is nothing at all.

---

**Sources consulted:**

- Isaacson, Walter. *Elon Musk.* Simon & Schuster, 2023.
- Dodd, Tim. "Starbase Tour with Elon Musk [Part 1 // Summer 2021]." Everyday Astronaut, August 3, 2021.
- Hightower, Ray. "Elon Musk and the Algorithm." RayHightower.com, September 28, 2023.
- ModelThinkers. "Musk's 5 Step Design Process."
- Corporate Rebels. "Musk's 5 Step Algorithm to Cut Internal Bureaucracy at Tesla." February 9, 2026.
- Startup Archive. "Elon Musk explains his 5-step algorithm for running companies."
- Haden, Jeff. "Elon Musk's 'Algorithm,' a 5-Step Process to Dramatically Improve Nearly Anything." Inc., September 27, 2023.

---

*Generated by Claude (Anthropic). Reviewed and edited by a human for accuracy, tone, and applicability. Last updated April 2026.*
